# Deep Research

Use this skill when the user wants a thorough, web-grounded answer rather than a quick response.

## Research Workflow

1. Restate the research goal in one sentence.
2. If the topic is too broad or the expected output is unclear, ask one focused clarification. Otherwise proceed.
3. Build a search plan with 3-6 angles, such as:
   - official or primary sources
   - recent news and analysis
   - technical documentation or papers
   - government, standards, legal, or regulatory sources
   - company pages, filings, changelogs, or product docs
   - expert blogs, forums, GitHub issues, benchmarks, or community reports
4. Use `WebSearch` for broad discovery and `WebFetch` for sources that look useful.
5. Prefer primary, official, recent, and technically specific sources. Use secondary sources for context, not as the only proof.
6. Cross-check important claims with at least two independent sources when possible.
7. Track source quality, publication date, author or organization, and any conflicts between sources.
8. Do not cite sources you did not inspect. Do not invent facts, dates, numbers, quotes, or URLs.

## Source Strategy

Use a mix of source types when relevant:

- Official documentation, API references, standards, regulatory pages, or product pages
- Academic papers, whitepapers, patents, and technical reports
- Government datasets, court records, financial filings, and policy documents
- Reputable journalism, industry analysis, and analyst reports
- GitHub repositories, release notes, issue trackers, mailing lists, and forums
- Company blogs, engineering blogs, case studies, pricing pages, and changelogs
- Reviews, benchmarks, social discussions, and user reports when sentiment or adoption matters

Treat low-authority sources as signals to investigate, not final evidence.

## Analysis Standards

- Separate facts from interpretation.
- Call out uncertainty, outdated information, weak evidence, or conflicting claims.
- Include dates for time-sensitive claims.
- Compare alternatives using criteria that matter to the user's question.
- Prefer a direct recommendation when the evidence supports one.
- If the web results are thin, say so and explain the best available conclusion.

## Final Output

Structure the answer for decision-making:

```markdown
## Executive Summary
[Best answer in 2-5 concise bullets or one short paragraph.]

## Key Findings
- [Finding with source link]
- [Finding with source link]
- [Finding with source link]

## Analysis
[Synthesize what the evidence means. Explain tradeoffs, patterns, and contradictions.]

## Recommendation
[Clear recommendation or best answer, with assumptions.]

## Sources
- [Source title](URL) — why it matters
- [Source title](URL) — why it matters
```

For small questions, compress the structure. For complex or data-heavy topics, add sections such as methodology, comparison, timeline, risks, or open questions.

content.text:
# Deep Research: The People of Trilogy Innovations and Their Intellectual Footprint

## Executive Summary

- **Important disambiguation:** "Trilogy Innovations" maps to **at least three unrelated companies**. Based on your workspace (references to `github.com/trilogy-group`, "Trilogy University," "Coding Intern," and the `innovations.trilogy.com` domain), the relevant one is the **Bengaluru-based Trilogy Innovations (formerly CodeNation Innovation Labs)**, which is part of **Joe Liemandt's Trilogy / ESW Capital** software empire — *not* the West Virginia government-contracting firm of the same name.
- The Bengaluru entity explicitly markets its **"innovative processes and tools" as its core Intellectual Property (IP)** and runs **Trilogy University**, a 3-month onboarding "gauntlet" modeled on US Navy SEAL training. It acts as the **R&D arm** for 30+ acquired enterprise products.
- The intellectual center of gravity is **Joe Liemandt** (Stanford dropout, founder of Trilogy Software in 1989). His enduring intellectual contributions are: (1) the **product configurator / pricing engine** that became "the first AI product to sell a billion dollars," (2) **Trilogy University** as a talent-and-R&D model later echoed by Google/Facebook bootcamps, and (3) the **ESW Capital / Crossover** operating model of acquiring software firms and running them with globally distributed contractors.
- The most concrete "intellectual property" is a **patent portfolio** (via Versata, acquired 2006), famous for the **Versata v. SAP** case — a $391M jury verdict whose key patent (**US 6,553,350** on hierarchical pricing) was ultimately **invalidated** as an abstract idea.

---

## Key Findings

### 1. Three different "Trilogy Innovations" exist — don't conflate them

| Entity | HQ | Founded | People | Relevant to your workspace? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Trilogy Innovations (CodeNation)** | Bengaluru, India | 2014 | Vipul Sanghi (Partner); part of Liemandt's Trilogy | **Yes** — `innovations.trilogy.com`, Trilogy University, "Coding Intern" |
| **Trilogy / Trilogy Software** | Austin, TX | 1989 | Joe Liemandt (founder/CEO); parent of the above | Yes (parent ecosystem; `trilogy-group` on GitHub) |
| **Trilogy Innovations, Inc.** | Bridgeport, WV | 2010 | Brandon Downey, Randy Cottle | **No** — unrelated US gov-contracting firm |

The West Virginia firm is a separate, SBA 8(a) minority-owned IT-services company led by Brandon Downey and Randy Cottle ([trilogyit.com](https://www.trilogyit.com/about-trilogy)). It has no connection to Liemandt. I'm setting it aside below.

### 2. The Bengaluru entity (the one in your files): people + IP claim

- Founded 2014 as **CodeNation Innovation Labs**, rebranded to **Trilogy Innovations**; ~31 employees, presence in US/UK/Uzbekistan; listed executive **Vipul Sanghi (Partner)** ([LinkedIn](https://in.linkedin.com/company/trilogyinnovations)).
- Self-describes as *"the World's First Team to apply Disruptive thinking to Enterprise Software Challenges… built ground up on the basis of innovative processes and tools that are our Intellectual Property (IP)."* It is *"practically the R&D arm of our clients,"* developing 30+ enterprise products ([AeroLeads profile](https://aeroleads.com/c/trilogyinnovations), [CB Insights](https://www.cbinsights.com/company/trilogy-innovations)).
- **Trilogy University (TU):** *"conceptualized and modeled from the 'US Navy SEAL Training Program'… an incubation environment… to transform student recruits into thorough problem solvers in 3 months."* This is the same talent pipeline your workspace "Coding Intern" / "problem-solving coach" transcripts sit inside.

### 3. Joe Liemandt — the intellectual core

- Stanford dropout (1989); built Trilogy on **product configurators** — software that lets a salesperson translate complex customer needs into a buildable, correctly priced spec on the spot (preventing salespeople from selling un-buildable configurations) ([HBR PDF hosted by Trilogy](https://innovations.trilogy.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/09/HBR-Entreprunership.pdf), [Liemandt interview](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=smCNYsBzg0g)).
- Trilogy's **SalesBuilder** was branded a landmark **AI** system; by 1998 Trilogy was ~$150M revenue, ~$1B valuation; Liemandt twice on the Forbes cover and the youngest self-made member of the Forbes 400 in 1996 ([Colossus: "Class Dismissed"](https://colossus.com/article/joe-liemandt-class-dismissed/), [Wikipedia: Joe Liemandt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joe_Liemandt)).
- Now runs two arms: **Trilogy** (organic build) and **ESW Capital** (acquisitions), plus the **Crossover** talent platform, and is funding **Alpha Schools**, an AI-driven K-12 education venture.

### 4. Trilogy University — the most-copied intellectual artifact

- A near-mythological **"100-day, 100-hour-a-week gauntlet."** New hires formed teams, built a prototype + business model, and **Liemandt "played VC,"** funding ~15% of projects — using TU as a primary **R&D engine** (1995–2001: TU projects produced ~$25M revenue and seeded ~$100M in new business) ([Colossus](https://colossus.com/article/joe-liemandt-class-dismissed/)).
- Reported to have **inspired similar onboarding bootcamps at Google and Facebook**, and is a staple Harvard/business-school **case study** on recruiting and culture ([Scribd case](https://www.scribd.com/document/539365605/Case-1-Trilogy)).

### 5. The literal Intellectual Property: the Versata patent saga

- Trilogy acquired **Versata** (2006) and folded in Trilogy Technology Group, offshoring most engineering to Bangalore ([Wikipedia: Versata](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Versata)).
- **Versata v. SAP:** Versata sued SAP (2007) over **US Patent 6,553,350** (hierarchical product/organizational pricing). A jury awarded **$345M**, affirmed on appeal to **~$391M with interest** ([Reuters](https://www.reuters.com/article/technology/us-court-invalidates-versata-patent-in-391-million-case-idUSKCN0PJ2LS/)).
- But the **PTAB invalidated the patent** in the first-ever **Covered Business Method** review, and the **Federal Circuit upheld** invalidation (2015), ruling the "invention" was **not a technological one** — *"akin to creating organizational management charts"* ([Federal Circuit opinion](https://storage.courtlistener.com/pdf/2015/07/09/versata_development_group_v._sap_america_inc..pdf), [Google Patents](https://patents.google.com/patent/US6553350)).
- Forbes documented Liemandt building a **patent-litigation "war chest,"** suing ~20 companies (SAP, Sears, Toyota, Ford) via Versata ([Forbes investigation](https://www.forbes.com/sites/nathanvardi/2018/11/19/how-a-mysterious-tech-billionaire-created-two-fortunesand-a-global-software-sweatshop/)).

---

## Analysis

There are really two senses of "intellectual" at play, and the evidence supports both:

1. **Intellectual capital / ideas (people-driven):** The defining intellectual contributions of "Trilogy" are *organizational and methodological*, not just code: the **configurator-as-AI** product insight, and the **Trilogy University** model of using elite-graduate bootcamps simultaneously as a hiring filter, a culture seeder, and an R&D incubator. The Bengaluru "Trilogy Innovations" is a direct continuation of this — it explicitly frames its **processes/tools as proprietary IP** and runs its own TU. Your workspace transcripts (interns being graded on judgment, framing, tradeoffs, pushback) are essentially TU's "problem-solver" philosophy operationalized through Cursor agents.

2. **Formal intellectual property (legal):** Here the record is more cautionary than celebratory. The flagship patent (`'350`) generated a massive verdict but was ultimately struck down as an abstract idea, and the broader strategy drew "patent troll" / "software sweatshop" criticism in mainstream press. So the *legal* IP legacy is contested.

**Contradiction worth flagging:** Sources differ on the CodeNation→Trilogy relationship. LinkedIn/CB Insights/AeroLeads treat "Trilogy Innovations" as simply the **rebrand** of CodeNation Innovation Labs under the `trilogy.com` umbrella (consistent with Liemandt's roll-up model and the `innovations.trilogy.com` domain). One search synthesis claimed "no public record of acquisition." The domain, branding, Trilogy University, and "R&D arm for client's 30+ acquired products" language all strongly tie it to Liemandt's Trilogy, but I could not find a primary acquisition document to date/confirm the exact mechanism.

**Caveat on individuals:** Beyond Liemandt and Vipul Sanghi, I did not find authoritative, verifiable named rosters of *current* Bengaluru Trilogy Innovations engineers from primary sources (only scattered individual LinkedIn profiles such as a "Senior SDE, formerly CodeNation"). LinkedIn employee names are unverified and I won't list them as fact.

---

## Recommendation / Best Answer

If you're asking *"who works at Trilogy Innovations and what is their intellectual footprint?"*, the accurate answer is:

- The relevant company is **Trilogy Innovations (CodeNation), Bengaluru**, an arm of **Joe Liemandt's Trilogy / ESW Capital**. Its public "face" person is **Vipul Sanghi (Partner)**, and its intellectual identity is built around **Trilogy University** and **proprietary development processes/tools it brands as IP**.
- The **intellectual lineage** flows from **Joe Liemandt** and original Trilogy Software: the **product configurator ("first $1B AI product")**, the **TU talent/R&D model**, and a **patent portfolio (Versata)** whose marquee asset was invalidated.

If instead you meant the **West Virginia Trilogy Innovations, Inc.**, the key people are **Brandon Downey (CEO)** and **Randy Cottle (President/COO)**, with a leadership bench including Craig Molina (CTO) and Miles Kelsey (CIO) — a separate, unrelated firm.

Tell me which of the three you meant (or if you want me to focus on **named individual engineers**, the **patent portfolio in depth**, or the **Trilogy University methodology**), and I'll go deeper.

---

## Sources

- [About | Trilogy (WV firm)](https://www.trilogyit.com/about-trilogy) — leadership of the unrelated West Virginia company.
- [Trilogy Innovations (Bengaluru) — LinkedIn](https://in.linkedin.com/company/trilogyinnovations) — CodeNation rebrand, Vipul Sanghi, `innovations.trilogy.com`.
- [Trilogy Innovations — CB Insights](https://www.cbinsights.com/company/trilogy-innovations) — R&D arm, 30+ products, Trilogy University.
- [AeroLeads — Trilogy Innovations](https://aeroleads.com/c/trilogyinnovations) — the company's own "processes and tools are our IP" + Navy SEAL TU description.
- [Wikipedia: Joe Liemandt](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joe_Liemandt) & [Trilogy (company)](https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trilogy_(company)) — founder bio, corporate structure.
- [Colossus: "Class Dismissed"](https://colossus.com/article/joe-liemandt-class-dismissed/) — Trilogy University as gauntlet + R&D engine; AI/SalesBuilder history.
- [HBR "No Ordinary Boot Camp" (hosted by Trilogy)](https://innovations.trilogy.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/09/HBR-Entreprunership.pdf) — configurator value proposition.
- [Forbes: "…Two Fortunes and a Global Software Sweatshop"](https://www.forbes.com/sites/nathanvardi/2018/11/19/how-a-mysterious-tech-billionaire-created-two-fortunesand-a-global-software-sweatshop/) — ESW/Crossover model, patent war chest.
- [Reuters: Versata patent invalidated](https://www.reuters.com/article/technology/us-court-invalidates-versata-patent-in-391-million-case-idUSKCN0PJ2LS/) & [Federal Circuit opinion](https://storage.courtlistener.com/pdf/2015/07/09/versata_development_group_v._sap_america_inc..pdf) & [US 6,553,350 (Google Patents)](https://patents.google.com/patent/US6553350) — the formal IP and its fate.

One open question before I go further: **which Trilogy did you mean**, and do you want the next pass focused on *named individuals*, the *patent/IP portfolio*, or the *Trilogy University intellectual methodology*?

# High-Bar Portfolio Entry Rubric (Trilogy Innovations)

Use this rubric to identify only the rare transcript-backed moments that deserve to become portfolio entries. The unit of judgment is not a single prompt. The unit is the full thinking episode: prior context, user turns, assistant responses, artifact changes, and the latest outcome.

Default label is `below_bar`. Use `above_bar` only when the episode proves a non-obvious human judgment move that a third-party reviewer could use to assess the person's intellectual capability.

## Who This Is For: Trilogy Innovations

This rubric grades the work of people inside Trilogy Innovations, where the baseline talent is already high. Recruits pass through Trilogy University, a problem-solving gauntlet modeled on elite training programs, and the culture rewards a specific kind of thinker: someone who applies disruptive, first-principles thinking to enterprise-software problems instead of accepting the conventional path. Trilogy people operate as the R&D arm for their clients, manage massive scale at low cost without sacrificing quality, and treat AI as the core leverage that makes that possible.

Because the population is already strong, the bar for `above_bar` must be raised, not lowered. A move that would impress from an average engineer can still be `below_bar` here if it only shows competent execution. The portfolio exists to surface the rare moves that separate an exceptional Trilogy problem-solver from a merely capable one.

Trilogy-defining traits the portfolio should capture:

- Disruptive, first-principles reframing: rejecting "how it is normally done" and redesigning from the real constraint, scale, or cost target. Conventional, textbook-correct answers are not enough.
- Founder-grade ownership: the person drives the decision the way an owner would, not the way a ticket-taker would. At Trilogy University recruits own a product end to end while the founder plays VC; the portfolio should show that same ownership of the problem and its outcome.
- Leverage at scale: using AI, automation, deterministic workflows, or reuse to get large output, cost savings, and quality at the same time. This is the opposite of conventional development, where scale usually costs quality and money.
- Business value: connecting the engineering move to a real outcome (revenue, cost, client problem, risk, time-to-value), not just technical elegance.
- AI as a force multiplier, not a crutch: directing, correcting, and bounding the model while keeping human judgment in control of the decision.
- Evidence over assertion: demanding proof, concrete constraints, and measurable bars before committing, consistent with a meritocracy that measures everything.

## Purpose

Use this rubric to identify only the rare transcript-backed conversations that deserve to become portfolio entries.

The unit of judgment and the unit that gets labeled is the whole conversation, never a single prompt or a single turn. A conversation is the full thread: every user turn, every assistant response, the artifacts it changed, and the latest outcome. You assign exactly one `label` and one `score` to the conversation as a whole.

Default label is `below_bar`. Use `above_bar` only when the conversation as a whole proves a non-obvious human judgment move that a third-party reviewer could use to assess the person's intellectual capability.

## Unit of Judgment: The Conversation

Grade conversations, not prompts. Do not pick out a single prompt and label it; read the whole conversation and decide whether the conversation earns `above_bar`.

- A conversation is `above_bar` when, read end to end, it demonstrates qualifying human judgment. This is normally anchored by one or more clearly user-owned high-bar moves that are sustained across the thread and visible in the artifact or outcome.
- One strong move can carry a conversation, but only if the rest of the conversation does not undercut it (for example, the user then hands all the thinking back to the assistant, or abandons the insight). A single strong prompt inside an otherwise routine conversation is `transcript_highlight`, not `above_bar`.
- A conversation with many competent but ordinary turns and no rare move is `below_bar`, no matter how long or how polished the individual prompts are.
- When citing evidence for the label, quote the specific turns that drove it, but the verdict still belongs to the conversation, not to those turns in isolation.

content.text: Now the Labels and Hard Gates sections:

input.old_string:
- `above_bar` - Portfolio-worthy. The episode can become a real portfolio entry with minimal editing because it proves original human judgment, context, artifact impact, and learning.
- `transcript_highlight` - Useful moment, strong prompt, or good collaboration pattern, but not enough for a portfolio entry.
- `below_bar` - Routine, generic, assistant-led, or insufficiently evidenced.

Only `above_bar` should be used for final portfolio entries. `transcript_highlight` may be used for coaching or raw evidence, but it should not be presented as portfolio work.

## Hard Gates

If any gate is missing, the episode is not `above_bar`.

- Full-context judgment: the reviewer inspected the surrounding conversation and can explain how the user's thinking evolved.
- Human-original move: the user introduced the key insight, concern, reframing, standard, or decision; it was not supplied first by the assistant.
- Assistant-delta proof: the user changed the direction, quality, or standard of the assistant's work rather than only accepting, formatting, or requesting expansion.
- Real project stakes: the episode is grounded in a specific product, workflow, user, client, system, artifact, or business decision.
- Non-obvious insight: the move is not something a generic LLM would likely produce from the docs with a prompt like "write a good insight."
- Artifact impact: the thinking changed or meaningfully shaped an IPD, ITD, architecture, research conclusion, implementation path, review standard, or decision.
- Evidence and constraint grounding: the user used or demanded concrete evidence, domain constraints, failure cases, API limits, stakeholder needs, or project-specific details.
- Reflection or learning: the episode shows what became clearer, what assumption changed, what risk was exposed, or what should happen next.

# Deep Research

Use this skill when the user wants a thorough, web-grounded answer rather than a quick response.

## Research Workflow

1. Restate the research goal in one sentence.
2. If the topic is too broad or the expected output is unclear, ask one focused clarification. Otherwise proceed.
3. Build a search plan with 3-6 angles, such as:
   - official or primary sources
   - recent news and analysis
   - technical documentation or papers
   - government, standards, legal, or regulatory sources
   - company pages, filings, changelogs, or product docs
   - expert blogs, forums, GitHub issues, benchmarks, or community reports
4. Use `WebSearch` for broad discovery and `WebFetch` for sources that look useful.
5. Prefer primary, official, recent, and technically specific sources. Use secondary sources for context, not as the only proof.
6. Cross-check important claims with at least two independent sources when possible.
7. Track source quality, publication date, author or organization, and any conflicts between sources.
8. Do not cite sources you did not inspect. Do not invent facts, dates, numbers, quotes, or URLs.

## Source Strategy

Use a mix of source types when relevant:

- Official documentation, API references, standards, regulatory pages, or product pages
- Academic papers, whitepapers, patents, and technical reports
- Government datasets, court records, financial filings, and policy documents
- Reputable journalism, industry analysis, and analyst reports
- GitHub repositories, release notes, issue trackers, mailing lists, and forums
- Company blogs, engineering blogs, case studies, pricing pages, and changelogs
- Reviews, benchmarks, social discussions, and user reports when sentiment or adoption matters

Treat low-authority sources as signals to investigate, not final evidence.

## Analysis Standards

- Separate facts from interpretation.
- Call out uncertainty, outdated information, weak evidence, or conflicting claims.
- Include dates for time-sensitive claims.
- Compare alternatives using criteria that matter to the user's question.
- Prefer a direct recommendation when the evidence supports one.
- If the web results are thin, say so and explain the best available conclusion.

## Final Output

Structure the answer for decision-making:

```markdown

[Best answer in 2-5 concise bullets or one short paragraph.]

[Synthesize what the evidence means. Explain tradeoffs, patterns, and contradictions.]

## Recommendation
[Clear recommendation or best answer, with assumptions.]

## Sources
- [Source title](URL) — why it matters

```

For small questions, compress the structure. For complex or data-heavy topics, add sections such as methodology, comparison, timeline, risks, or open questions.

input.contents:
# High-Bar Portfolio Conversation Rubric (Trilogy Innovations)

`rubric_version: 2.0` - conversation-native rebuild. Set `rubric_version` in every graded output to match.

This rubric replaces the prompt-based rubric, which is archived verbatim at `data/rubric_v1.txt`. The filename is kept for link compatibility, but the unit of judgment is now the conversation, not the prompt.

Purpose: identify the rare AI-assisted conversations that prove a Trilogy person made a real, non-obvious judgment move worth keeping as a portfolio case study. The default verdict is `below_bar`. Most competent conversations are not portfolio-worthy.

## Design Basis

This rubric is grounded in current evaluation research, not intuition:

- Evaluate the whole conversation (trajectory), not isolated turns, and combine a holistic verdict with analytic, per-dimension diagnosis. This hybrid is the 2026 industry standard for multi-turn evaluation (Confident AI, DeepEval).
- Quality must be sustained across the trajectory: a conversation with five strong turns and one collapse has still failed (DeepEval; DynSess session-level judging).
- Guard against score inflation as context grows; longer transcripts tend to be over-scored (DynSess).
- Expert vs novice software judgment is visible in how a developer steers AI: seniors delegate strategically, keep control through detailed delegation and verification, expand scope to business context, anticipate second-order effects, protect system invariants, and own outcomes; juniors over-rely or defensively resist (HCI/SE field studies; practitioner reports).
- Analytic rubrics give diagnostic feedback; holistic rubrics and comparative judgment give reliable verdicts; anchor exemplars and inter-rater checks are required for consistency (HEQCO rubric guide; analytic-vs-holistic literature).

Sources are listed at the end.

## Who This Is For: Trilogy Innovations

This grades people inside Trilogy Innovations, where baseline talent is already high (Trilogy University problem-solver culture: disruptive, first-principles thinking; R&D arm operating at massive scale, low cost, and high quality; AI as the core leverage). Because the population is strong, competent execution is the baseline, not the achievement. Raise the bar; do not lower it. The portfolio captures the rare moves that separate an exceptional Trilogy problem-solver from a merely capable one.

## The Unit: A Conversation Is a Trajectory

A conversation is the full thread: every user turn, every assistant response, the artifacts it touched, and the latest outcome. You assign exactly one `label` and one `depth_score` to the conversation as a whole.

- Judge the arc, not a snapshot. What matters is how the user steered the work across turns: what they introduced, challenged, corrected, and decided as the thread developed.
- Sustained quality rule: a strong move that the user later undercuts (hands all thinking back to the model, abandons the insight, accepts a regression) caps the conversation at `transcript_highlight`.
- A single strong move can carry a conversation, but only if that move plus its surrounding context can fill the full case-study anatomy (situation, stakes, human move, evidence, alternatives rejected, artifact change, outcome). A lone clever one-liner with thin context is `transcript_highlight`.
- Multi-topic threads: if a conversation covers several unrelated tasks, judge it by its strongest self-contained work episode and record the turns that anchor it. Still one label for the conversation.
- Anti-inflation rule: length, number of turns, politeness, and prompt formatting never raise the score. A long, busy conversation with no rare move is `below_bar`.

## How To Read a Conversation

1. Read the whole thread start to finish.
2. Scan with a sliding window of about 3 to 5 turns to locate candidate high-judgment moments and identify the single strongest episode.
3. For each candidate moment, read the turns immediately before and after it to confirm who introduced the insight (user or assistant).
4. Inspect any artifact the conversation changed: IPD, ITD, design doc, code, research note, architecture decision, test, plan, or review.
5. Read the latest outcome, because the value may only be visible once the thread develops.

Never grade from a single prompt or excerpt unless the full conversation is unavailable. If it is unavailable, set `mentor_review_needed = true` and avoid `above_bar` unless the excerpt is overwhelming.

## Labels

- `above_bar` - Portfolio-worthy. The conversation can become a real case study with minimal editing because it proves original human judgment, real stakes, artifact impact, and learning, sustained across the trajectory.
- `transcript_highlight` - A genuine strong moment or good collaboration pattern, but not enough as a whole conversation for a portfolio entry. Useful for coaching and raw evidence; never published as portfolio work.
- `below_bar` - Routine, generic, assistant-led, inflated by length, or insufficiently evidenced.

## Evaluation Procedure

Apply in order. Gates are pass/fail eligibility; dimensions are an analytic quality score; the label is the holistic verdict.

1. Locate the strongest episode and key turns (sliding window).
2. Check the eligibility gates. Any gate failing makes `above_bar` impossible.
3. Score the ten dimensions across the trajectory to get a `depth_score` from 0 to 10.
4. Run the trajectory checks (sustained quality, recovery, anti-inflation).
5. Confirm the Trilogy bar.
6. Assign one label from the mapping.

## Eligibility Gates

Pass/fail. Not scored. If any gate fails, the best possible label is `transcript_highlight`. Gates may overlap with dimensions by design: the gate is the floor (present at all), the dimension measures how strongly.

- Full-conversation judgment: the reviewer read the whole thread and can explain how the user's thinking evolved.
- Human-original move: somewhere in the conversation the user introduced the key insight, concern, reframing, standard, or decision; it was not supplied first by the assistant.
- Sustained assistant-delta: across the trajectory the user changed the direction, quality, or standard of the assistant's work, not just once and not only by accepting, formatting, or asking for expansion.
- Real project stakes: grounded in a specific product, workflow, client, system, artifact, or business decision.
- Non-obvious insight: the strongest move is not something a generic LLM would likely produce from the docs with a prompt like "write a good insight."

## Scoring Dimensions

Score the conversation as a whole. Award 1 point only when the dimension is clearly and substantively demonstrated somewhere in the trajectory. No half credit. Do not award points for keyword presence, politeness, length, or a tidy prompt. Sum to a `depth_score` of 0 to 10.

Group A - Judgment and steering (the human move)

1. `ownership` - The user owns the decision like an owner, not a ticket-taker, and consciously decides what to own versus delegate to the model.
2. `originality` - The insight is non-obvious, specific, and unlikely to be produced by a generic LLM from the source docs alone.
3. `reframe` - The user reframes the problem, success standard, or architecture direction from first principles rather than accepting the conventional default.
4. `steering_recovery` - Across turns the user redirects, corrects, or bounds the assistant, including catching and recovering from a wrong or shallow assistant direction.

Group B - Engineering and business substance

5. `evidence` - The user provides, demands, challenges, or applies concrete facts, constraints, or measurable bars.
6. `alternatives` - The conversation compares meaningful paths or rejects options with reasons, including build vs buy vs integrate.
7. `tradeoffs_second_order` - The user makes cost, risk, scale, reversibility, or timing explicit and considers second-order effects or system invariants, not just the immediate change.
8. `leverage_at_scale` - The user uses AI, automation, deterministic workflows, or reuse to multiply output, cost-efficiency, and quality at once, without outsourcing judgment.
9. `business_value` - The move connects to a real outcome: client problem, revenue, cost, risk, or time-to-value, not only technical elegance.

Group C - Outcome

10. `artifact_impact` - The conversation materially changed a durable work product or decision: IPD, ITD, design, architecture, plan, implementation, review standard, or research conclusion.

## Scoring Anchors

The population is strong, so "good for a normal engineer" sits on the `0` side.

- `0` - Missing, generic, assistant-supplied, purely implied, conventional or textbook-correct without a distinctive angle, or visible only with charity.
- `1` - Specific, user-owned, grounded in the transcript, and strong enough to change how a skilled Trilogy collaborator would understand or continue the work.

If you cannot quote or closely paraphrase the user's move for a dimension, score it `0`.

## Trajectory Checks

Apply after scoring, before labeling.

- Sustained quality: if a strong early move is undercut later in the thread, cap at `transcript_highlight`.
- Recovery credit: catching a wrong assistant trajectory and steering it back is strong evidence for `steering_recovery` and `ownership`.
- Anti-inflation: re-confirm that the score reflects rare judgment, not transcript volume. Trim any point that only looks earned because the conversation is long.

## From Score to Label

1. If any eligibility gate fails, the conversation cannot be `above_bar`.
2. Compute `depth_score` (0 to 10).
3. Confirm the Trilogy bar: the conversation shows disruptive or first-principles thinking, founder-grade ownership, leverage at scale, AI used as a multiplier, or a clear line to business value, beyond competent execution.
4. Assign one label:
   - `above_bar` - all gates pass, Trilogy bar cleared, sustained-quality check passed, and `depth_score >= 8`.
   - `transcript_highlight` - not `above_bar`, but there is at least one genuine user-owned move of real value, and either `depth_score` is 4 to 7 or a single gate is the only blocker.
   - `below_bar` - `depth_score <= 3`, no genuine user-owned move, or more than one gate fails.

A score of 8 or 9 should be a clear, defensible entry. Reserve a perfect 10 for conversations that feel exceptional even among strong Trilogy problem-solvers. On a boundary, choose the lower label.

## Insight Types

Classify the conversation's strongest move as one of:

- `root_problem_reframe` - turns the apparent task into the real underlying problem.
- `first_principles_disruption` - rejects the conventional approach and rebuilds from the underlying constraint, scale, or cost target.
- `assistant_correction` - catches a wrong, shallow, or overconfident assistant direction.
- `domain_specific_edge_case` - finds a case that breaks the generic solution because of project-specific workflow or domain rules.
- `build_vs_buy_judgment` - narrows what to buy, build, integrate, or reject based on differentiated value.
- `architecture_boundary_design` - defines boundaries, ownership, data flow, failure modes, or integration shape.
- `leverage_design` - designs AI, automation, or deterministic workflows that multiply output and quality at scale.
- `business_value_link` - ties a technical move to a client outcome, revenue, cost, risk, or time-to-value.
- `evidence_standard_raise` - demands proof, source validation, POC criteria, or measurable decision evidence.
- `scope_and_tradeoff_control` - prevents over- or under-building by naming the right depth, phase, or constraint.
- `artifact_transformation` - turns messy discussion into a durable decision artifact.
- `principled_pushback` - rejects a plausible path because it violates the real goal, user need, or review standard.

If none appears anywhere in the conversation, it is usually `below_bar`.

## Automatic Fail Patterns

A conversation is not `above_bar` if its overall arc matches any of these, unless it also contains a rare human-original move that carries the whole thread.

- Mostly asking the assistant to write an IPD, ITD, plan, research note, or market analysis.
- Dominated by rewrite, formatting, tone, summarization, or "make this better."
- User turns are prompt-engineering templates (goal, context, constraints, output format) with no real judgment.
- Information retrieval where the insight is in the assistant's research, not the user's judgment.
- Asking for options without ever naming the user's own hypothesis, constraint, rejection, or decision.
- Accepting the assistant's recommendation without validation, pushback, or a changed standard.
- Turns that sound strong only because they contain words like "tradeoff," "risk," "architecture," or "evidence."
- Generic architecture or product questions that could apply to many projects.
- A portfolio story that can only be told by inventing context not present in the transcript.
- The assistant's contribution is consistently more impressive than the user's.
- The strongest user move is ordinary project management, not distinctive intellectual judgment.
- Accepts the conventional approach without testing whether Trilogy's scale, cost, or AI-leverage model demands a different design.
- Adds manual steps or an unreliable LLM-in-the-loop where a deterministic or automated workflow was the higher-leverage move.
- Technically sound but with no line to business value, client outcome, or cost and scale impact.
- One good prompt inside an otherwise routine thread; this is `transcript_highlight`.
- High score driven by conversation length or volume rather than a rare move.

## Required Diagnosis Fields

Each grade is for one conversation and must include:

- `conversation_ref` - identifier or path of the conversation.
- `label` - `above_bar`, `transcript_highlight`, or `below_bar`.
- `depth_score` - integer 0 to 10, the sum of the ten dimensions.
- `dimension_scores` - the per-dimension 0/1 breakdown.
- `hard_gates_failed` - list of failed eligibility gates, or `none`.
- `trilogy_bar_cleared` - true or false, with one line on which trait it shows.
- `insight_type` - the strongest insight type, or `none`.
- `key_turns` - the specific turns that anchor the verdict.
- `strongest_episode` - one-line description of the episode graded, for multi-topic threads.
- `context_chain` - what happened in the conversation before its strongest move.
- `assistant_delta` - what the user added beyond prior assistant output, across the trajectory.
- `human_original_move` - the user's strongest original contribution.
- `artifact_impact` - what changed in the work product or decision path.
- `sustained_quality` - whether quality held across the thread or was undercut later.
- `why_not_generic_llm` - why this was not a generic model-generated insight.
- `missing_move` - the highest-leverage addition that would make it portfolio-worthy.
- `mentor_review_needed` - true when context, originality, or domain meaning is uncertain.

## Portfolio Entry Anatomy

An `above_bar` conversation must be convertible into a case study with all of these. If any cannot be filled honestly from the transcript, it is not a portfolio entry.

- `situation` - what was happening before the move.
- `stakes` - why it mattered.
- `human_move` - what the user uniquely noticed, challenged, decided, or reframed.
- `evidence_used` - the facts, constraints, APIs, docs, or failures that grounded it.
- `alternatives_rejected` - the plausible path avoided or replaced.
- `artifact_changed` - the IPD, ITD, design, architecture, plan, implementation, or standard that changed.
- `outcome_or_learning` - what became clearer, safer, faster, more correct, or more strategic.
- `why_not_generic_llm` - why this required context-specific human judgment.

## Coaching Requirements

For `below_bar` and `transcript_highlight` conversations, coach toward a better thinking conversation, not just a better prompt:

- Name the failed gates or the weak dimensions.
- State whether the weakness is missing context, ownership, assistant-supplied insight, missing artifact impact, missing evidence, missing outcome, missing reflection, or quality not sustained across the thread.
- Tie the explanation to specific turns in the conversation.
- Give a stronger next move: a validation question, an edge-case test, a POC criterion, a decision log, an artifact revision, a second-order-effect check, or an evidence demand.

Do not turn a weak conversation into a polished fake portfolio entry. If the conversation does not prove the insight, say so.

## Calibration

Treat the rubric as a measuring instrument.

- Maintain a mentor-labeled dataset, one row per conversation: `conversation_ref`, `key_turns`, `strongest_episode`, `prior_context_summary`, `artifact_before`, `artifact_after`, `mentor_label`, `mentor_depth_score`, `mentor_reason`, `hard_gates_failed`, `expected_insight_type`, `rubric_version`.
- Keep anchor conversations: at least one labeled exemplar for `above_bar`, `transcript_highlight`, and `below_bar`, plus a borderline pair. Anchors improve inter-rater reliability.
- For borderline conversations, use comparative judgment: rank the candidate against the nearest anchor instead of arguing the absolute score, since pairwise comparison is more reliable for tacit quality.
- Audit false positives first. Re-run the non-obvious and sustained-quality checks on every approved entry.
- Closed loop: when a misgrade is found, capture the failure pattern and add it as an automatic-fail example or an anchor, then re-version the rubric.
- Not production-grade until at least 50 real conversations are mentor-labeled across obvious failures, highlights, borderline cases, and true `above_bar` entries.

Targets:

- zero known false positives in approved entries.
- mentor agreement on `above_bar` decisions above 90 percent (inter-rater reliability).
- every disagreement categorized by a failed gate, a contested dimension, or ambiguous originality.

## Source Principles

- Portfolio entries are case studies of judgment, not prompt collections.
- The unit is the conversation trajectory; sustained judgment beats a single clever turn.
- Strong AI use is necessary but not sufficient; competent execution is the baseline, the rare move is the achievement.
- The key question is not "was the prompt good?" but "across this conversation, did the person make a rare, context-specific intellectual move that reflects how a Trilogy problem-solver thinks?"
- Reject aggressively. A few undeniable entries beat a long list of polished but ordinary conversations.

## Sources

- [Multi-Turn LLM Evaluation in 2026 - Confident AI](https://www.confident-ai.com/blog/multi-turn-llm-evaluation-in-2026) - holistic plus sliding-window turn evaluation; conversation completeness as the primary metric.
- [Multi-Turn Evaluation - DeepEval](https://deepeval.com/guides/guides-multi-turn-evaluation) - sustained quality across the whole conversation, not single turns.
- [DynSess: Dynamic Session-Level Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.29256) - trajectory-level scoring and score inflation as context grows.
- [Evaluating Multi-turn Human-AI Interaction (TCR)](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.18660) - transparency, consistency, refinement across turns.
- [Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.22458) - end-to-end, memory, and planning evaluation goals.
- [Agency in AI-assisted software engineering (arXiv 2602.00496)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.00496) - senior vs junior judgment, strategic delegation, control, second-order effects.
- [AI isn't replacing junior devs - DEV Community](https://dev.to/aws/ai-isnt-replacing-junior-devs-your-org-chart-is-1edp) - expand scope, own systems, engage business context and trade-offs.
- [HEQCO Guide to Valid and Reliable Rubrics](https://heqco.ca/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Formatted_Rubric-Guide_FINAL.pdf) - analytic vs holistic, inter-rater reliability, anchor assignments.
- [Analytic vs Holistic Rubrics](https://blog.alludolearning.com/analytic-vs-holistic-rubric) and [DePaul Teaching Commons](https://resources.depaul.edu/teaching-commons/teaching-guides/feedback-grading/rubrics/Pages/types-of-rubrics.aspx) - when to use each; comparative judgment for reliability.

Done. Here's the deep-research summary and what I built from it.

## Executive Summary
- Archived the old rubric verbatim to `data/rubric_v1.txt`, then **rebuilt `data/high_bar_prompt_rubric.md` as a conversation-native v2.0**, grounded in 2026 multi-turn evaluation research, AI-assisted-engineering judgment studies, and rubric-design literature.
- The new design is a **hybrid**: holistic conversation verdict + analytic per-dimension diagnosis + sliding-window turn location — which is exactly what current multi-turn evaluation practice recommends.

## Key Findings (research → design)
- **Evaluate the trajectory, not turns.** Industry tooling (Confident AI, DeepEval) and research (DynSess, TCR) score the whole session and warn that single-turn scoring misses the point → I made the conversation arc the unit and added a "Conversation Is a Trajectory" section.
- **Sustained quality matters.** "Five great turns and one collapse is still a failure" (DeepEval) → added a **sustained-quality rule** that caps undercut conversations at `transcript_highlight`.
- **Score inflation grows with context.** Longer transcripts get over-scored (DynSess) → added an explicit **anti-inflation rule and trajectory check**.
- **Expert vs novice judgment is about steering AI.** SE field studies show seniors delegate strategically, keep control, expand scope to business context, anticipate second-order effects, protect invariants, and own outcomes → became the **`steering_recovery`, `ownership` (with strategic delegation), `tradeoffs_second_order`, and `business_value`** dimensions.
- **Rubric methodology:** analytic rubrics for diagnosis, holistic + comparative judgment for reliable verdicts, anchor exemplars + inter-rater checks for consistency (HEQCO) → added **anchor conversations, comparative judgment for borderline cases, and a 90% inter-rater target**.

## What changed vs v1
- Title/unit now **conversation**, not prompt; sliding-window read procedure added.
- Dimensions reorganized into 3 research-backed groups (Judgment/Steering, Substance, Outcome), with new `steering_recovery` and `tradeoffs_second_order`.
- New **Design Basis** and **Sources** sections (citations inline).
- Kept the coherent v2 machinery you approved: 5 eligibility gates, 0–10 depth score, Trilogy bar, score→label mapping (`above_bar` ≥8 + gates + sustained quality).
- Diagnosis fields gained `strongest_episode`, `sustained_quality`, `dimension_scores`.

## Notes / decisions I made
- **Filename kept** as `high_bar_prompt_rubric.md` for link compatibility (referenced by `Abdul's.md`/`Arleif's.md`/`IPD.md`), with an in-file note that it's now conversation-based. Say the word if you'd prefer I rename it to `high_bar_conversation_rubric.md` and update references.
- Threshold stayed at **depth ≥ 8**; I recommend we validate it against Abdul/Arleif and recalibrate rather than guess.

## Sources
- [Multi-Turn LLM Evaluation 2026 — Confident AI](https://www.confident-ai.com/blog/multi-turn-llm-evaluation-in-2026), [DeepEval Multi-Turn](https://deepeval.com/guides/guides-multi-turn-evaluation) — holistic + sliding-window, sustained quality.

# High-Bar Portfolio Window Rubric (Trilogy Innovations)

`rubric_version: 2.1` - window-based unit, read in full-conversation context. Set `rubric_version` in every graded output to match.

This rubric supersedes the prompt-based rubric (archived verbatim at `data/rubric_v1.txt`). The filename is kept for link compatibility.

Purpose: find the rare moments in AI-assisted work where a Trilogy person made a real, non-obvious judgment move worth keeping as a portfolio case study. The default verdict is `below_bar`.

## The Unit: A Window, Read in Full-Conversation Context

This is the most important rule. There are two different roles for the transcript:

- The conversation is context. Read the whole thread so you can tell who introduced an insight, what the assistant already said, how the thinking evolved, and what artifact changed.
- The window is the unit you grade, label, and return. A window is the strongest self-contained contiguous segment of turns that holds one high-judgment episode. You do not grade or return the entire conversation, and you do not grade a single isolated prompt.

So: read the whole conversation, but return the window alone.

- A window is normally located with a sliding scan of about 3 to 5 turns, then trimmed to the smallest contiguous span that still makes a standalone case study: the user's move plus the assistant turn that set it up and the assistant turn that responds. Typically 2 to 6 turns.
- A conversation can contain more than one qualifying window. Return each qualifying window as its own entry. If only the strongest is wanted, return that one. Never collapse them into a single label on the whole conversation.
- A window earns a label on its own merits, but only the full conversation can confirm the assistant-delta (that the user, not the assistant, introduced the move). Use the conversation to verify; attach the verdict to the window.
- Anti-inflation: do not widen a window to inflate its score. Extra turns that do not add judgment do not add points. Length of the conversation never raises a window's score.

## Design Basis

Grounded in current evaluation research, not intuition:

- Multi-turn evaluation uses a sliding window of about 3 to 5 turns over the conversation; you assess a turn (or short span) using the surrounding turns as context. This is the 2026 standard (Confident AI, DeepEval). The window is exactly that span.
- Score the trajectory of the window, not one keystroke; quality must hold across the window, not collapse mid-move (DeepEval; DynSess).
- Guard against score inflation as context grows; longer transcripts get over-scored (DynSess). Hence the window, not the whole thread, is the scored unit.
- Expert vs novice judgment shows in how a developer steers AI: seniors delegate strategically, keep control through detailed delegation and verification, expand scope to business context, anticipate second-order effects, protect system invariants, and own outcomes; juniors over-rely or defensively resist (HCI/SE field studies).
- Analytic dimensions give diagnostic feedback; holistic plus comparative judgment give reliable verdicts; anchor exemplars and inter-rater checks are required for consistency (HEQCO; analytic-vs-holistic literature).

Sources are listed at the end.

## Who This Is For: Trilogy Innovations

This grades people inside Trilogy Innovations, where baseline talent is already high (Trilogy University problem-solver culture: disruptive, first-principles thinking; R&D arm at massive scale, low cost, high quality; AI as the core leverage). Competent execution is the baseline, not the achievement. Raise the bar. The portfolio captures the rare windows that separate an exceptional Trilogy problem-solver from a merely capable one.

## How To Grade

1. Read the whole conversation start to finish for context.
2. Scan with a sliding window of about 3 to 5 turns to locate candidate high-judgment episodes.
3. For each candidate, trim to the smallest contiguous window that stands alone as a case study, and read the turns just before and after to confirm who introduced the insight.
4. Inspect any artifact the window changed: IPD, ITD, design doc, code, research note, architecture decision, test, plan, or review.
5. Run gates, dimensions, checks, and the Trilogy bar on the window (using the conversation as context).
6. Label and return the window or windows. Never label the whole conversation.

If the full conversation is unavailable, set `mentor_review_needed = true` and avoid `above_bar` unless the window itself is overwhelming.

## Labels

Each label applies to a window, not the conversation.

- `above_bar` - Portfolio-worthy. The window can become a real case study with minimal editing because it proves original human judgment, real stakes, artifact impact, and learning.
- `transcript_highlight` - A genuine strong moment, but not enough on its own for a portfolio entry. Useful for coaching and raw evidence; never published as portfolio work.
- `below_bar` - Routine, generic, assistant-led, or insufficiently evidenced.

## Evaluation Procedure

Apply to the window. Gates are pass/fail eligibility; dimensions are an analytic quality score; the label is the holistic verdict.

1. Confirm the window boundaries (start turn, end turn).
2. Check the eligibility gates. Any gate failing makes `above_bar` impossible.
3. Score the ten dimensions over the window to get a `depth_score` from 0 to 10.
4. Run the window checks (sustained move, recovery, anti-inflation).
5. Confirm the Trilogy bar.
6. Assign one label to the window.

## Eligibility Gates

Pass/fail. Not scored. If any gate fails, the best possible label is `transcript_highlight`. Gates may overlap with dimensions: the gate is the floor (present at all), the dimension measures how strongly.

- Context verified: the reviewer read the surrounding conversation and can explain how the window's move arose.
- Human-original move: in the window the user introduced the key insight, concern, reframing, standard, or decision; it was not supplied first by the assistant (verified against prior turns).
- Assistant-delta: the user changed the direction, quality, or standard of the assistant's work, not only by accepting, formatting, or asking for expansion.
- Real project stakes: the window is grounded in a specific product, workflow, client, system, artifact, or business decision.
- Non-obvious insight: the move is not something a generic LLM would likely produce from the docs with a prompt like "write a good insight."

## Scoring Dimensions

Score the window (using the conversation as context). Award 1 point only when the dimension is clearly and substantively demonstrated in the window. No half credit. Do not award points for keyword presence, politeness, length, or a tidy prompt. Sum to a `depth_score` of 0 to 10.

Group A - Judgment and steering (the human move)

1. `ownership` - The user owns the decision like an owner, not a ticket-taker, and consciously decides what to own versus delegate to the model.
2. `originality` - The insight is non-obvious, specific, and unlikely to be produced by a generic LLM from the source docs alone.
3. `reframe` - The user reframes the problem, success standard, or architecture direction from first principles rather than accepting the conventional default.
4. `steering_recovery` - In the window the user redirects, corrects, or bounds the assistant, including catching and recovering from a wrong or shallow assistant direction.

Group B - Engineering and business substance

5. `evidence` - The user provides, demands, challenges, or applies concrete facts, constraints, or measurable bars.
6. `alternatives` - The window compares meaningful paths or rejects options with reasons, including build vs buy vs integrate.
7. `tradeoffs_second_order` - The user makes cost, risk, scale, reversibility, or timing explicit and considers second-order effects or system invariants, not just the immediate change.
8. `leverage_at_scale` - The user uses AI, automation, deterministic workflows, or reuse to multiply output, cost-efficiency, and quality at once, without outsourcing judgment.
9. `business_value` - The move connects to a real outcome: client problem, revenue, cost, risk, or time-to-value, not only technical elegance.

Group C - Outcome

10. `artifact_impact` - The window materially changed a durable work product or decision: IPD, ITD, design, architecture, plan, implementation, review standard, or research conclusion.

## Scoring Anchors

The population is strong, so "good for a normal engineer" sits on the `0` side.

- `0` - Missing, generic, assistant-supplied, purely implied, conventional or textbook-correct without a distinctive angle, or visible only with charity.
- `1` - Specific, user-owned, grounded in the transcript, and strong enough to change how a skilled Trilogy collaborator would understand or continue the work.

If you cannot quote or closely paraphrase the user's move for a dimension, score it `0`.

## Window Checks

Apply after scoring, before labeling.

- Sustained move: the judgment must hold across the window, not collapse mid-move. If the user makes a strong move and then immediately hands all thinking back or abandons it within the window, cap at `transcript_highlight`.
- Recovery credit: catching a wrong assistant direction and steering it back is strong evidence for `steering_recovery` and `ownership`.
- Anti-inflation: re-confirm the score reflects rare judgment, not how many turns the window or conversation contains. Trim any point earned only by volume.

## From Score to Label

1. If any eligibility gate fails, the window cannot be `above_bar`.
2. Compute `depth_score` (0 to 10).
3. Confirm the Trilogy bar: the window shows disruptive or first-principles thinking, founder-grade ownership, leverage at scale, AI used as a multiplier, or a clear line to business value, beyond competent execution.
4. Assign one label to the window:
   - `above_bar` - all gates pass, Trilogy bar cleared, sustained-move check passed, and `depth_score >= 8`.
   - `transcript_highlight` - not `above_bar`, but there is a genuine user-owned move of real value, and either `depth_score` is 4 to 7 or a single gate is the only blocker.
   - `below_bar` - `depth_score <= 3`, no genuine user-owned move, or more than one gate fails.

A score of 8 or 9 should be a clear, defensible entry. Reserve a perfect 10 for windows that feel exceptional even among strong Trilogy problem-solvers. On a boundary, choose the lower label.

## Insight Types

Classify the window's strongest move as one of:

- `root_problem_reframe` - turns the apparent task into the real underlying problem.
- `first_principles_disruption` - rejects the conventional approach and rebuilds from the underlying constraint, scale, or cost target.
- `assistant_correction` - catches a wrong, shallow, or overconfident assistant direction.
- `domain_specific_edge_case` - finds a case that breaks the generic solution because of project-specific workflow or domain rules.
- `build_vs_buy_judgment` - narrows what to buy, build, integrate, or reject based on differentiated value.
- `architecture_boundary_design` - defines boundaries, ownership, data flow, failure modes, or integration shape.
- `leverage_design` - designs AI, automation, or deterministic workflows that multiply output and quality at scale.
- `business_value_link` - ties a technical move to a client outcome, revenue, cost, risk, or time-to-value.
- `evidence_standard_raise` - demands proof, source validation, POC criteria, or measurable decision evidence.
- `scope_and_tradeoff_control` - prevents over- or under-building by naming the right depth, phase, or constraint.
- `artifact_transformation` - turns messy discussion into a durable decision artifact.
- `principled_pushback` - rejects a plausible path because it violates the real goal, user need, or review standard.

If none appears in the window, it is usually `below_bar`.

## Automatic Fail Patterns

A window is not `above_bar` if it matches any of these, unless it also contains a rare human-original move.

- The user is mainly asking the assistant to write an IPD, ITD, plan, research note, or market analysis.
- Rewrite, formatting, tone, summarization, or "make this better."
- The user turn is a prompt-engineering template (goal, context, constraints, output format) with no real judgment.
- Information retrieval where the insight is in the assistant's research, not the user's judgment.
- Asking for options without naming the user's own hypothesis, constraint, rejection, or decision.
- Accepting the assistant's recommendation without validation, pushback, or a changed standard.
- The window sounds strong only because it contains words like "tradeoff," "risk," "architecture," or "evidence."
- A generic architecture or product question that could apply to many projects.
- A portfolio story that can only be told by inventing context not present in the transcript.
- The surrounding conversation shows the assistant introduced the insight first, so the assistant-delta fails.
- The strongest move is ordinary project management, not distinctive intellectual judgment.
- Accepts the conventional approach without testing whether Trilogy's scale, cost, or AI-leverage model demands a different design.
- Adds manual steps or an unreliable LLM-in-the-loop where a deterministic or automated workflow was the higher-leverage move.
- Technically sound but with no line to business value, client outcome, or cost and scale impact.
- A window widened or a score inflated by turn volume rather than a rare move.

## Required Diagnosis Fields

Each grade is for one window and must include:

- `conversation_ref` - identifier or path of the source conversation (context).
- `window_turns` - the turn range of the window (start turn to end turn) and a short excerpt or quote of the user's move.
- `label` - `above_bar`, `transcript_highlight`, or `below_bar`.
- `depth_score` - integer 0 to 10, the sum of the ten dimensions.
- `dimension_scores` - the per-dimension 0/1 breakdown.
- `hard_gates_failed` - list of failed eligibility gates, or `none`.
- `trilogy_bar_cleared` - true or false, with one line on which trait it shows.
- `insight_type` - the strongest insight type, or `none`.
- `context_chain` - what happened in the conversation before the window.
- `assistant_delta` - what the user added beyond prior assistant output, verified against surrounding turns.
- `human_original_move` - the user's original contribution in the window.
- `artifact_impact` - what changed in the work product or decision path.
- `sustained_move` - whether the judgment held across the window or collapsed mid-move.
- `why_not_generic_llm` - why this was not a generic model-generated insight.
- `missing_move` - the highest-leverage addition that would make the window portfolio-worthy.
- `mentor_review_needed` - true when context, originality, or domain meaning is uncertain.

## Portfolio Entry Anatomy

An `above_bar` window must be convertible into a case study with all of these. If any cannot be filled honestly from the window plus its context, it is not a portfolio entry.

- `situation` - what was happening before the move.
- `stakes` - why it mattered.
- `human_move` - what the user uniquely noticed, challenged, decided, or reframed.
- `evidence_used` - the facts, constraints, APIs, docs, or failures that grounded it.
- `alternatives_rejected` - the plausible path avoided or replaced.
- `artifact_changed` - the IPD, ITD, design, architecture, plan, implementation, or standard that changed.
- `outcome_or_learning` - what became clearer, safer, faster, more correct, or more strategic.
- `why_not_generic_llm` - why this required context-specific human judgment.

## Coaching Requirements

For `below_bar` and `transcript_highlight` windows, coach toward a better thinking move, not just a better prompt:

- Name the failed gates or the weak dimensions.
- State whether the weakness is missing context, ownership, assistant-supplied insight, missing artifact impact, missing evidence, missing outcome, or a move that collapsed mid-window.
- Tie the explanation to the specific turns in the window.
- Give a stronger next move: a validation question, an edge-case test, a POC criterion, a decision log, an artifact revision, a second-order-effect check, or an evidence demand.

Do not turn a weak window into a polished fake portfolio entry. If the window does not prove the insight, say so.

## Calibration

Treat the rubric as a measuring instrument.

- Maintain a mentor-labeled dataset, one row per window: `conversation_ref`, `window_turns`, `prior_context_summary`, `artifact_before`, `artifact_after`, `mentor_label`, `mentor_depth_score`, `mentor_reason`, `hard_gates_failed`, `expected_insight_type`, `rubric_version`.
- Keep anchor windows: at least one labeled exemplar for `above_bar`, `transcript_highlight`, and `below_bar`, plus a borderline pair. Anchors improve inter-rater reliability.
- For borderline windows, use comparative judgment: rank the candidate against the nearest anchor instead of arguing the absolute score.
- Audit false positives first. Re-run the non-obvious and assistant-delta checks on every approved window.
- Closed loop: when a misgrade is found, capture the pattern as an automatic-fail example or an anchor, then re-version the rubric.
- Not production-grade until at least 50 real windows are mentor-labeled across obvious failures, highlights, borderline cases, and true `above_bar` entries.

Targets:

- zero known false positives in approved entries.
- mentor agreement on `above_bar` decisions above 90 percent (inter-rater reliability).
- every disagreement categorized by a failed gate, a contested dimension, or ambiguous originality.

## Source Principles

- The unit returned and labeled is the window; the conversation is only context.
- Portfolio entries are case studies of judgment, not prompt collections.
- Strong AI use is necessary but not sufficient; competent execution is the baseline, the rare move is the achievement.
- The key question is not "was the prompt good?" but "in this window, did the person make a rare, context-specific intellectual move that reflects how a Trilogy problem-solver thinks?"
- Reject aggressively. A few undeniable windows beat a long list of polished but ordinary ones.

## Sources

- [Multi-Turn LLM Evaluation in 2026 - Confident AI](https://www.confident-ai.com/blog/multi-turn-llm-evaluation-in-2026) - sliding-window turn evaluation (3 to 5 turns) plus holistic metrics.
- [Multi-Turn Evaluation - DeepEval](https://deepeval.com/guides/guides-multi-turn-evaluation) - sustained quality across turns, sliding window.
- [DynSess: Dynamic Session-Level Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.29256) - trajectory scoring and score inflation as context grows.
- [Evaluating Multi-turn Human-AI Interaction (TCR)](https://arxiv.org/html/2605.18660) - transparency, consistency, refinement across turns.
- [Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.22458) - end-to-end, memory, and planning evaluation goals.
- [Agency in AI-assisted software engineering (arXiv 2602.00496)](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.00496) - senior vs junior judgment, strategic delegation, control, second-order effects.
- [AI isn't replacing junior devs - DEV Community](https://dev.to/aws/ai-isnt-replacing-junior-devs-your-org-chart-is-1edp) - expand scope, own systems, engage business context and trade-offs.
- [HEQCO Guide to Valid and Reliable Rubrics](https://heqco.ca/wp-content/uploads/2020/06/Formatted_Rubric-Guide_FINAL.pdf) - analytic vs holistic, inter-rater reliability, anchor assignments.
- [Analytic vs Holistic Rubrics](https://blog.alludolearning.com/analytic-vs-holistic-rubric) and [DePaul Teaching Commons](https://resources.depaul.edu/teaching-commons/teaching-guides/feedback-grading/rubrics/Pages/types-of-rubrics.aspx) - when to use each; comparative judgment for reliability.
